symptomSummary: After a prompt revision accumulates ~30 invocations, the evolve loop stops fixing current failures and instead produces revisions that address older issues or are measurably worse than the prior version.
rootCauseHypothesis: The feedback retrieval query fetches all historical invocation records for the artifact without ordering by recency or capping to a window, so as total invocation count grows the older (majority) records statistically drown out the recent thumbs-down signals that should be driving the next revision.
fixHypothesis: The repository method that loads invocations/feedback for the evolve prompt should ORDER BY timestamp DESC with a LIMIT N (e.g. 20–50 most recent) or apply exponential recency weighting before assembling the feedback list passed to the enhancer agent, so recent negative signals dominate older ones.

primaryLocation.rationale: Threshold-shaped failures that appear only after sufficient data accumulates almost always live in the query/repository layer. This is the sole SQLite data-access module; a SELECT without ORDER BY timestamp DESC + LIMIT means every new invocation dilutes recent signal. The symptom resets on rollback (fresh revision, zero history) and recurs once traffic rebuilds — exactly what an unwindowed full-table scan produces.
primaryLocation.function: getInvocations or getFeedback (whichever method supplies records to the evolve pipeline)

candidateLocations.rationale: This service assembles the context (feedback records + revision history) passed to the enhancer agent. Even if the repository returns all rows, a recency cap or weight could be applied here. Its absence is the second most likely root cause.

The enhancer agent constructs the actual LLM prompt driving revision generation. If it concatenates all supplied feedback without recency sorting, recent thumbs-down events are buried in a long list of older signals.
Entry point for the evolve operation — if it fetches or forwards an unbounded invocation list downstream, this is where the window cap should be applied first.
The public evolve() API surface may contain intermediate orchestration logic that selects which invocations to surface; a missing recency filter here would affect all downstream consumers.
